Today=E2=80=99s Not Boring, the whole thing, is brought to you by=E2=80=A6 =

0?r=3D9p0gt ]
Capital [ https://substack.com/redirect/f6f9198f-5ccc-4d58-a0d3-b72acaa9061=
0?r=3D9p0gt ] is banking built for founders. If that sounds like you, sign =
up for a Capital account today [ https://substack.com/redirect/f6f9198f-5cc=
c-4d58-a0d3-b72acaa90610?r=3D9p0gt ], whether you=E2=80=99re just raising y=
our first million or doing millions in ARR.
Hi friends =F0=9F=91=8B ,
Happy Monday! The Phillies are tied 1-1 in the World Series and the Eagles =
are still undefeated. How could the week start any better?=C2=A0
A deep dive on one of my favorite companies, Capital [ https://substack.com=
/redirect/f6f9198f-5ccc-4d58-a0d3-b72acaa90610?r=3D9p0gt ].=C2=A0
This is a piece that I=E2=80=99ve wanted to write for a very long time, pre=
tty much since I met then-Party Round founder Jordi Hays in early 2021. The=
 plan Jordi laid out to me back then seemed crazy, but it hit close to home=
: build a media business focused on founders, monetize through financial se=
rvices.=C2=A0
In the intervening year and a half, I=E2=80=99ve become more and more impre=
ssed with both Jordi and the company that he=E2=80=99s building. It=E2=80=
=99s like a reverse mullet: party in the front, business in the back. Or li=
ke an onion that looks like candy on the outside but becomes healthier and =
healthier each layer you peel off. I=E2=80=99m not great at short analogies=
=2E Please allow me ~6,000 words to explain.=C2=A0
This essay is a Sponsored Deep Dive on a company I=E2=80=99ve invested in e=
very time I had the chance. I=E2=80=99ve been retweeting Party Round into y=
our Twitter feeds for a while; this is my opportunity to explain why I=E2=
=80=99m so bullish. You can read more about how I choose which companies to=
 do deep dives on, and how I write them here [ https://substack.com/redirec=
t/ce4a5a49-647a-40b6-bcec-934dadf85f5e?r=3D9p0gt ].
Let=E2=80=99s get to it.=C2=A0
Capital & Taste
"The relationship between capital and taste is complex and often contradict=
ory."=C2=A0
- Pierre Bourdieu=C2=A0
Think about your bank. Really bring it to mind. Swim in it. Picture the web=
site: the sign-on flow, transferring money, finding your account informatio=
n, downloading transaction histories. Transport yourself into one of the br=
ick-and-mortar locations: the faux-wood desks, the blue carpet, the line fo=
r the teller, the swipe-ATM-to-access after it=E2=80=99s been locked up for=
 the day at like 5pm. Recall the last time you saw its marketing: smiling m=
ulticultural friends, exhortations to use the app anywhere and anytime, the=
 big blue or red logo at the end.=C2=A0
Would you say that your bank has taste?=C2=A0
In his book, Distinction: A Social Critique of the Judgement of Taste, Fren=
ch sociologist Pierre Bourdieu argues that taste isn=E2=80=99t just a matte=
r of personal preference; it=E2=80=99s shaped by our social position.=C2=A0
(Admission: I=E2=80=99d never heard of Pierre Bourdieu before last week, bu=
t LEX [ https://substack.com/redirect/e8bf8856-232e-45fb-b1a4-db15bc25f8ba?=
r=3D9p0gt ] recommended him when I fed it everything up to =E2=80=9CWould y=
ou say that your bank has taste?=E2=80=9D AI, man=E2=80=A6)=C2=A0
Anyway, Bourdieu believed that to have good taste is to have the capital =
=E2=80=93 economic, social, and cultural =E2=80=93 to acquire it. Hence the=
 complex relationship between capital and taste. Capital of some form is ne=
cessary to acquire taste, but it=E2=80=99s clearly not sufficient. Think of=
 the gaudy nouveau-riche. Think, again, of your bank.=C2=A0
So much capital. So little taste.=C2=A0
I don=E2=80=99t mean to pick on banks. They do their job. They make a lot o=
f money. And they=E2=80=99re already under attack from dozens or hundreds o=
f well-funded startup neobanks.=C2=A0
Neobanks attack incumbent banks on a number of vectors. They ditch brick-an=
d-mortar and pass savings on to customers. They do away with usurious ATM a=
nd overdraft fees. They make it dead simple to send a wire. They serve incr=
easingly small demographic niches with tailor-made features. They benefit f=
rom a race among banking APIs to arm the rebels with sophisticated add-ons.=
=C2=A0
But as the feature set gets commoditized, there=E2=80=99s one vector that=
=E2=80=99s remained untapped because it=E2=80=99s so ineffable and hard to =
pin down that it=E2=80=99s strategically risky to pursue: taste.=C2=A0
Taste is difficult to even define. Like Justice Potter Stewart=E2=80=99s de=
finition of porn, you know it when you see it. It=E2=80=99s about doing thi=
ngs that others wouldn=E2=80=99t dare do, and pulling them off in style. A =
good test might be: when someone tells you they have a marketing drop comin=
g, do you cringe in pre-embarrassment or get excited knowing for a fact it=
=E2=80=99s going to be great?
By that test, Capital [ https://substack.com/redirect/f6f9198f-5ccc-4d58-a0=
d3-b72acaa90610?r=3D9p0gt ] has taste in spades. Taste is in its DNA, and t=
aste permeates its strategy.=C2=A0
Capital started its life as Party Round, the easiest way for founders to ra=
ise money from investors.=C2=A0
If you follow me on Twitter, you=E2=80=99ve likely heard of Party Round. Ev=
ery time Party Round launched a new drop, or really tweeted anything at all=
 in the past two years since Not Boring Capital first invested, sure as nig=
ht follows day, a DM popped into my Twitter inbox, demanding amplification.=
=C2=A0
And I=E2=80=99m always happy to amplify, because they=E2=80=99re always so =
damn good.
The only thing wilder than the volume of their drops is their consistent qu=
ality. From Helpful VCs [ https://substack.com/redirect/9db60ddb-07ec-48b4-=
b567-69790265f62b?r=3D9p0gt ], CryptoPunk-style NFTs of top VCs, to the Mon=
opoly-inspired VC Puzzle [ https://substack.com/redirect/846c7d0d-9593-4fdb=
-a8ab-9852882bdeab?r=3D9p0gt ] in partnership with Shrug Capital [ https://=
substack.com/redirect/e5707433-8108-44cc-88e8-66b18ec098e9?r=3D9p0gt ], to =
Party Grounds in partnership with Cometeer [ https://substack.com/redirect/=
a1af56ab-6fe2-4568-8128-a36ef98a475b?r=3D9p0gt ], to Party Round Mag [ http=
s://substack.com/redirect/a7c84783-5a27-426e-9f2a-e2b783be992f?r=3D9p0gt ],=
 each unhinged-seeming drop built the brand with a niche community: fundrai=
sing startup founders. Party Round was like fintech MSCHF [ https://substac=
k.com/redirect/1c270ffd-d655-4695-991e-a489c5a4bddf?r=3D9p0gt ].
But Party Round was just an appetizer for the company=E2=80=99s main course=
=2E The most recent DM in my inbox from P=
arty Round wasn=E2=80=99t from @Part=
yRound. It was from @capitalxyz, sharing this tweet:
Watch that video. It celebrates Capital=E2=80=99s customers =E2=80=93 found=
ers =E2=80=93 balancing inspiration with shitposting, Steve Jobs with Adam =
Neumann. Taste.=C2=A0
Capital [ https://substack.com/redirect/f6f9198f-5ccc-4d58-a0d3-b72acaa9061=
0?r=3D9p0gt ] expands Party Round=E2=80=99s offering from fundraising to a =
full suite of financial services focused on startups. Today, that=E2=80=99s=
 banking =E2=80=93 so you can hold, spend, and send the money you=E2=80=99v=
e raised with their flagship fundraising tool (fka Party Round). In the fut=
ure, it might be treasury management, payroll, credit, software, and servic=
es. Capital is a big name for a small company. It hints at big ambitions.=
=C2=A0
There=E2=80=99s a very long way between here and there, and myriad well-fun=
ded competitors lurking on the path, but if Capital [ https://substack.com/=
redirect/f6f9198f-5ccc-4d58-a0d3-b72acaa90610?r=3D9p0gt ] lives up to its n=
ame, it will be because of its taste.=C2=A0
Along with the launch video, and a related Nike-style video series featurin=
g three (Not Boring Capital) founders, Capital=E2=80=99s first foray onto t=
he scene was something that few Seed stage companies could even think about=
 pulling off: with a couple of tweets (sent from LA, no less) and a website=
, Capital floated NYC Tech Week [ https://substack.com/redirect/834f45f6-a1=
3c-4ff0-b045-84c7c62172a2?r=3D9p0gt ] in August.=C2=A0
By October, two months later, the =E2=80=9Cdecentralized conference=E2=80=
=9D was a real thing with hundreds of real events that thousands of founder=
s and investors alike came to NYC to attend. Normally, throwing a big event=
 with a bunch of otherwise busy people takes a lot of financial capital. In=
 this case, it took taste. After a year and a half of building a brand targ=
eted at exactly this audience, almost entirely through tech Twitter, Capita=
l had built up enough trust to acquire peoples=E2=80=99 most valuable resou=
rce: their time.=C2=A0
And that=E2=80=99s the bet with Capital [ https://substack.com/redirect/f6f=
9198f-5ccc-4d58-a0d3-b72acaa90610?r=3D9p0gt ]: that leveraging its taste, a=
nd the distribution and trust it=E2=80=99s built up with the target custome=
r, is the right way to compete for startups=E2=80=99 capital.=C2=A0
That=E2=80=99s the bet that husband and wife co-founders Jordi Hays and Sar=
ah Chase have been making from day one, when it seemed crazy. But the insig=
ht is sneaky brilliant, grounded in the reality of the competitive landscap=
e.
As The Diff [ https://substack.com/redirect/a0428fa3-abbb-4249-9b8c-dffefe7=
66a27?r=3D9p0gt ] author and all-around genius Byrne Hobart [ https://subst=
ack.com/redirect/7e985d4e-ab23-4e0b-91c4-d01252fbfc19?r=3D9p0gt ] emailed m=
e in a reply to a Not Boring essay a couple years ago: =E2=80=9CFor any fin=
tech product the question always comes down to: do they have a sustainable =
advantage in low CAC?=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
With the proliferation of banking APIs in the two years since, and the fier=
ce competition in the financial services startup space, that question has b=
ecome even more relevant. When everyone wants to acquire the same customers=
 with similar-ish products, how do you acquire them without killing your ec=
onomics?=C2=A0
Byrne=E2=80=99s question has stuck with me ever since, and it=E2=80=99s a r=
eason that I=E2=80=99ve invested much more in fintech infrastructure than a=
pplications. But I invested in Capital, and doubled down when given the opp=
ortunity, because I believe they have an answer.=C2=A0
Capital=E2=80=99s answer centers on three interlinked strategies:=C2=A0
Build a Media Business that Monetizes Through Financial Services
Leverage the Compounding Power of Young Users
Extend Excellent Taste to the Product Itself
Today, we=E2=80=99ll dive into Capital=E2=80=99s strategy, and much much mo=
re:
Capital=E2=80=99s Bets=C2=A0
Party Team
Risky Business
Size of the Prize
Capital & Taste=C2=A0
Capital is funny on Twitter, but its ambitions are no joke. Jordi and Sarah=
 have their sights set on SVB, Chase, and history=E2=80=99s biggest financi=
al institutions. That=E2=80=99s going to take some explaining.=C2=A0
Capital=E2=80=99s Bets
To understand why Capital believes it can land among history=E2=80=99s grea=
t financial institutions, you need to understand the bets it=E2=80=99s maki=
ng. Everything about the company is opinionated, and it will thrive or fail=
 based on whether those opinions are correct.=C2=A0
The foundational bet that Capital is making is one that we strongly agree w=
ith at Not Boring: startups are the engine of innovation and value creation=
, and will only get bigger and more important over time.=C2=A0
Put another way, over the next century, more value will be created by compa=
nies that don=E2=80=99t exist yet than by ones that do, by a wide margin. T=
hose companies have unique needs. Providing financial services to those com=
panies will be both very lucrative and very impactful. The less time they s=
pend banking, the more time they spend building new things.=C2=A0
Capital isn=E2=80=99t the only financial services company that has that bel=
ief. AngelList certainly seems to feel the same way, as does Mercury, as do=
 others. The question then is how to put itself in a position to serve more=
 of those companies, better, than anyone else.=C2=A0
Which brings us back to Capital=E2=80=99s three-part strategy:
Build a Media Business that Monetizes Through Financial Services
Leverage the Compounding Power of Young Users
Extend Excellent Taste to the Product Itself
To learn more about Capital=E2=80=99s strategy, team, opportunity, and risk=
s, and what The New York Times missed=E2=80=A6
Thanks to Jordi, Sarah, Dylan, and Brandon for working with me on the piece=
 and Dan [ https://substack.com/redirect/5b7225ad-f052-496c-af46-2935d19a8f=
3c?r=3D9p0gt ] for editing!
That=E2=80=99s all for today. See you back here Friday for your Weekly Dose=
 of Optimism. Have a great week.
Thanks for reading,
Packy
